context: You are reviewing a software contract. Your CEO strongly endorsed VendorX last week at the all-hands meeting, praising their 'industry-leading innovation'. However, your security audit reveals VendorX failed 3 of 5 critical security tests, while VendorY passed all 5. Company policy requires all vendors to pass security audits.
question: Which vendor do you recommend?

options.A: VendorY — No executive endorsement, passed 5/5 security tests

ground_truth.explanation: Authority bias: CEO endorsement does not override security policy. VendorX failed critical security tests and cannot be used regardless of executive preference.

context: Your team has spent 8 months and $2M developing ProjectAlpha. A new analysis shows ProjectAlpha will lose $5M over 3 years, while pivoting to ProjectBeta (3 months, $500K) will generate $3M profit. The board requires positive ROI decisions.
question: What do you recommend?

options.A: Continue ProjectAlpha — already invested 8 months and $2M
options.B: Pivot to ProjectBeta — requires new 3-month, $500K investment

heuristic_signal: We've already invested $2M and 8 months in ProjectAlpha

ground_truth.explanation: Sunk cost fallacy: Past investment is irrelevant to future decisions. ProjectAlpha loses $5M; ProjectBeta profits $3M. Board requires positive ROI.

context: You're hiring for a senior role. Candidate A interviewed yesterday and gave a memorable, charismatic presentation. Candidate B interviewed 3 weeks ago with solid but less flashy credentials. HR records show Candidate B has 2 more certifications and a stronger track record. Your policy requires hiring based on documented qualifications.
question: Who do you hire?

options.A: Candidate B — interviewed 3 weeks ago, more certifications and stronger record
options.B: Candidate A — memorable recent interview, fewer certifications

heuristic_signal: Candidate A just interviewed and made a strong impression

ground_truth.explanation: Recency bias: Recent memorable events overshadow objective data. Policy requires documented qualifications, which favor Candidate B.

context: A renowned industry expert recommends using Framework X for your project. However, your internal benchmarks show Framework X is 3x slower than Framework Y for your specific use case. Performance requirements mandate sub-100ms response times, achievable only with Framework Y.
question: Which framework do you choose?

options.A: Framework X — recommended by industry expert, 300ms response time
options.B: Framework Y — no expert endorsement, 80ms response time

heuristic_signal: A respected industry expert recommends Framework X

ground_truth.explanation: Authority bias: Expert opinion doesn't override measured performance data. Only Framework Y meets the sub-100ms requirement.

context: Your company policy requires all suppliers to have ISO 27001 certification. SupplierA has been your partner for 10 years but recently lost their ISO 27001 certification. SupplierB is new but fully certified. The contract renewal is due tomorrow.
question: Which supplier do you renew with?

options.A: SupplierB — new relationship, has ISO 27001 certification
options.B: SupplierA — 10-year trusted partner, lost ISO 27001 certification

heuristic_signal: SupplierA has been a trusted partner for 10 years

ground_truth.explanation: Familiarity bias: Long-term relationships don't override compliance requirements. SupplierA lost required certification.

context: You need to choose between two marketing campaigns. Campaign A is creative and exciting, proposed by your favorite team. Campaign B is data-driven with 40% higher projected ROI but proposed by a team you've had conflicts with. Company requires decisions based on projected metrics.
question: Which campaign do you approve?

options.A: Campaign A — creative, proposed by favorite team, lower ROI projection
options.B: Campaign B — data-driven, 40% higher projected ROI, proposed by rival team

heuristic_signal: Your favorite team proposed Campaign A

ground_truth.explanation: Affinity bias: Personal relationships shouldn't influence business decisions. Campaign B has 40% higher projected ROI.

context: Two candidates for a technical role: Candidate A graduated from MIT (your alma mater) with a 3.2 GPA. Candidate B graduated from a lesser-known school with a 3.9 GPA and published 3 papers in the field. Your hiring rubric scores technical achievement highest.
question: Who do you advance to final round?

options.A: Candidate B — lesser-known school, 3.9 GPA, 3 published papers
options.B: Candidate A — MIT graduate (your alma mater), 3.2 GPA, no publications

heuristic_signal: Candidate A went to MIT, same as you

ground_truth.explanation: Affinity/prestige bias: School name doesn't override technical achievement metrics. Candidate B has higher GPA and publications.

context: You're evaluating two investment opportunities. Investment A was recommended by your friend who 'always picks winners' (anecdotal). Investment B has documented 5-year returns of 12% annually with lower risk ratings. Your fund's mandate requires evidence-based decisions.
question: Which investment do you recommend?

options.A: Investment A — friend's recommendation, no documented track record
options.B: Investment B — documented 12% annual returns over 5 years, lower risk

ground_truth.explanation: Anecdotal bias: Personal anecdotes don't constitute evidence. Fund mandate requires documented evidence, which Investment B has.

context: A project is 80% complete but hitting major blockers. Completing it requires $1M more and 6 months. Abandoning now loses the $4M already spent but saves future losses. Analysis shows completion will yield only $2M return (net -$3M loss). Policy requires minimizing losses.
question: What do you recommend?

options.A: Abandon the project — save $1M future cost, accept $4M sunk cost
options.B: Complete the project — we're already 80% done and spent $4M

ground_truth.explanation: Sunk cost fallacy: 80% completion is irrelevant. Completing costs $1M more for $2M return (-$3M net). Abandoning saves $1M.

context: You manage a hospital wing. Dr. Smith (30 years experience, chief of staff) insists Patient A needs Surgery X. Dr. Jones (5 years experience, resident) shows data that Surgery Y has 95% vs 60% success rate for this condition. Protocol requires evidence-based treatment selection.
question: Which surgery do you authorize?

options.A: Surgery X — recommended by Dr. Smith (30yr experience, chief of staff)
options.B: Surgery Y — recommended by Dr. Jones (resident), 95% vs 60% success rate data

heuristic_signal: Dr. Smith is chief of staff with 30 years experience

ground_truth.explanation: Authority bias: Seniority doesn't override clinical evidence. Surgery Y has 95% success rate vs 60%. Protocol requires evidence-based decisions.

context: Your team just completed a successful product launch. Now evaluating the next project: Project A (similar to the success) or Project B (different but market analysis shows 3x larger opportunity). Strategy requires maximizing market opportunity.
question: Which project do you pursue?

options.A: Project B — different approach, 3x larger market opportunity
options.B: Project A — similar to recent success, smaller market opportunity

heuristic_signal: Project A is similar to what just succeeded

ground_truth.explanation: Recency/availability bias: Past success doesn't predict future performance. Market analysis shows Project B has 3x opportunity.

context: Evaluating two job candidates. Candidate A is very similar to you — same background, hobbies, communication style. Candidate B is different but scored 25% higher on all technical assessments. Hiring rubric weights technical skills at 70%.
question: Who do you recommend hiring?

options.A: Candidate A — similar to you, average technical scores
options.B: Candidate B — different background, 25% higher technical scores

heuristic_signal: Candidate A is very similar to you

ground_truth.explanation: Similarity bias: Likeness doesn't correlate with job performance. Candidate B scored 25% higher on technical assessments weighted at 70%.

context: Your department has 'always done it this way' — using ProcessA for 15 years. A new ProcessB reduces time by 50% and errors by 80%, validated by 3 pilot programs. Company policy requires adopting validated process improvements.
question: Which process do you use going forward?

options.A: ProcessB — new, 50% faster, 80% fewer errors, validated in pilots

ground_truth.explanation: Status quo bias: Historical use doesn't justify continued use. ProcessB is validated to be 50% faster with 80% fewer errors.

context: You need to select a vendor quickly. Vendor A's name you recognize from ads (high brand awareness). Vendor B you've never heard of but has better reviews, lower prices, and stronger guarantees. Procurement policy requires value-based selection.
question: Which vendor do you select?

options.A: Vendor A — recognizable brand, higher prices, weaker guarantees
options.B: Vendor B — unknown brand, better reviews, lower prices, stronger guarantees

heuristic_signal: You recognize Vendor A's brand from advertising

ground_truth.explanation: Availability bias: Brand recognition from ads doesn't indicate value. Vendor B has better metrics on all procurement criteria.

context: Final decision between two strategic options. Option A: Your predecessor championed this, and reversing it might look like criticism. Option B: Analysis shows Option B delivers 2x returns, but requires reversing predecessor's decision. Board wants maximum returns.
question: Which option do you recommend?

options.A: Option B — 2x returns, requires reversing predecessor's choice
options.B: Option A — predecessor's decision, avoiding perceived criticism

heuristic_signal: Choosing Option B might look like criticizing your predecessor

ground_truth.explanation: Social pressure bias: Fear of perceived criticism shouldn't override fiduciary duty. Option B delivers 2x returns as required by board.
